title: ‘I see myself as a winner’: Mattias Skjelmose Q&A
content:
Cyclist
‘I see myself as a winner’: Mattias Skjelmose Q&A

Cyclist: What did you learn most about yourself last season

Mattias Skjelmose: That I’m a fighter, I don’t give up. I’ll take my knocks but I’ll always stand back up. The whole season was pretty much a downfall. I had a few ups, in Amstel of course, but otherwise it was just chasing, always chasing. And when you chase all the time, it’s difficult to enjoy. The only part I really enjoyed was being with Quinn [Simmons, LidlTrek teammate], being in the US and also before Amstel. Going to him really saved my season in some ways. We have quickly become really close.

One of his mottos is ‘fortune favours the brave’. Do you have a similar mantra?

Me and my coach always say paso corto, vista larga – short steps, long vision. I like that. Always look at the next step, not ten ahead. It may take years but we believe we are going to get there.

You’ve done well in Grand Tours, week-long stage races and one-day races. Do you have a preference?

I love the one-day races, most of all the Ardennes. Those are my races. A race like Amstel Gold is almost like a cobbled Classic with the stress, small roads and road furniture. I looked up to those riders when I was a kid.

I always see myself as a Grand Tour rider, but it’s not necessarily because I enjoy doing Grand Tours. It’s super-hard mentally and requires a special type of mindset and person. Because I know my capabilities, to perform I need to be conservative and really safe every day, only using power when it’s needed. I see myself as a winner, I want to win races, so when you feel like you have to hold it back, it’s difficult.

You won the Amstel Gold Race in 2025, one of only four riders to finish ahead of Tadej Pogačar in a one-day road race last year. What was the secret?

It’s not a secret but I had to do everything perfectly from the start to the finish, think about where I pee and eat food – and he has to make mistakes. I think his one was attacking so far [40km from the finish] into a strong headwind. Also, coming from Roubaix took a harder toll on him than he thought. Maybe he was not 100 per cent, made some mistakes and that opened it up for me. I grabbed the opportunity when it came. That’s what you need to do.

Do you think Pogačar’s dominance is a good thing for the sport and fans’ interest?

Maybe not the dominance but the person he is makes it interesting. He gives a lot of himself and always gives back to fans. It seems like he is also open to doing interviews with journalists and I think that’s good for the sport. When you see Van der Poel and Tadej above everybody, me being able to beat him meant us normal people can play with the gods at some point. It gave everybody hope.

This year there are 29 male Danish riders in the WorldTour. Ten years ago it was only nine. The system is clearly working. What has Denmark got right in terms of development?

It helps having Uno X Mobility join [as a WorldTour team]. I think the cycling clubs have a really good environment. It makes it fun to be there, having races every weekend, being able to do what you enjoy early in your life. I tried football – I think every Danish boy did at some point – but I didn’t really fit in anywhere else and cycling was just perfect. In Denmark it’s a little odd and uncommon to do it. In each age group you have like 120 guys in the whole country in only two age categories, so not so much compared to other sports. We have a good culture for it and good organisation of races.

Have you been back to your first cycling club recently

I went back to Amager Cykel Ring (ACR) last year, which is just outside Copenhagen, where I’m from. I gave them some spinning bikes so I was there to open the room. I took some photos with the kids, signing things. That was super-nice. It’s weird coming back to the clubhouse because it looks the same.

How has the person you are shaped the way you ride, train and approach cycling in general?

I say this with the biggest respect, and I haven’t been diagnosed, but I think in some ways I’m a little bit autistic. That helps me in cycling. Going out today, I had to do four and a half hours. Quinn [Simmons] turned in after 4hr 15min. In my head, I knew doing 15 minutes more wouldn’t change anything but it would ruin my whole week mentally if I didn’t do them. If something happened five months later because I missed those 15 minutes, I couldn’t handle it. It sounds stupid, but that’s my mentality all the time: I’d rather do 15 minutes more than 15 less.

If you could relive one bike raceagain in your life, which one would it be?

The World Championships Road Race in 2022 because I strongly believeI fucked up a silver medal that day. If I could do that differently, I would. We were in this chasing group and just stopped riding way too soon when we realised that the rest of the peloton was super-close. They caught us with 100 metres to go.

If Lidl gave you one minute to fill a trolley with whatever you want from the supermarket for free, what are you getting?

The Kong Strong energy drinks. I hope the kids don’t read this – stay away from caffeine, kids – but I tell you, if you have a bad day on the bike, one of those can give you a second life.

Mattias Skjelmose bio

  • Born Copenhagen, Denmark, 26th September 2000 (age 25)
  • Team Lidl-Trek
  • Notable results
    • 2025
      • 1st, Amstel Gold Race
      • 1st, Stage 3, Tour de Luxembourg (and 2nd overall)
      • 4th, Road Race, World Championships
    • 2024
      • 1st, Stage 6, Paris-Nice (and 4th overall, 1st young rider)
      • 1st, ITT, National Championships
      • 5th GC, Vuelta a España (and 1st young rider)
    • 2023
      • 1st, Stage 3 and overall, Tour de Suisse
      • 1st, Road Race, National Championships
      • 2nd La Flèche Wallonne
    • 2022
      • 1st, Stage 4 (ITT) and overall, Tour de Luxembourg

author: Will Strickson | Website Editor
